narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 56.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because it aligns closely with successful students' averages. Your Year 1 decile of 2 had a smaller but still negative influence, again highlighting early lower-tier grades relative to the average of 5.71. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 35 (a bit under the 36.7 average), and full-time student status—which is common for 92.7% of applicants—played lesser roles in the decision. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your profile stands out for those lower decile rankings, which often correlate with challenges in sustaining high performance through law school and beyond. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law, where systemic barriers can impact rankings, but rather the model's data-driven focus on these patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps targeting LSAT prep for a higher score, and seeking mentorship from successful Black attorneys who navigated similar paths. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor for other schools. You're clearly driven, and with targeted improvements, you can rewrite this story. Keep pushing—many have turned early setbacks into bar-passing triumphs.
